coredata.dc:title: A Novel Multi-Scale Joint Approach for Estimating Lithium-ion Battery Model Parameters and SOC Considering Hysteresis Effect and Temperature
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Electrical Power & Energy Systems
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xinhui
Bai, Wenyuan
Xie, Shuyu
Wang, Jiatong
Sutanto, Danny
Muttaqi, Kashem M.
coredata.dc:description: Precise estimating of the state of charge (SOC) in lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries is crucial for effective energy management and safety assurance in electric vehicles. This paper proposes a novel multi-scale joint estimation method for model parameters and SOC to enhance estimation accuracy under temperature variations and hysteresis effects. First, an improved second-order RC equivalent circuit model is developed by incorporating temperature dependencies and hysteresis effects, where the hysteresis parameters are calibrated offline using a data-driven approach. Then, the joint estimation approach employs an adaptive forgetting factor recursive least squares (AFFRLS) algorithm to dynamically update model parameters during SOC estimation, thereby maintaining model fidelity across diverse temperature conditions (−10 °C to 50 °C). Finally, an extended Kalman filter (EKF) is implemented for SOC estimation based on the real-time updated model parameters. Experimental validation under DST, US06, and FUDS conditions demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving a maximum voltage prediction error of 0.0650 and a maximum SOC estimation error of 0.0092 across the full temperature range.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SOC
Lithium-ion battery
Multi-scale joint estimation
Hysteresis effect
AFFRLS
Model parameter identification
EKF algorithm

row.entry: Multiple feature-electrochemical thermal coupling modeling method with real-time coefficient correction
Innovative square root − untraced Kalman filtering strategy
Improved particle swarm optimization − adaptive square root cubature Kalman filter
Improved hysteresis- and temperature-dependent model and multi-scale joint estimation based on AFFRLS-EKF

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of errors of AFFRLS-EKF algorithms.

head.ce:title: A Novel Multi-Scale Joint Approach for Estimating Lithium-ion Battery Model Parameters and SOC Considering Hysteresis Effect and Temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, Shandong 255000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, Shandong, 255000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical, Computer and Telecommunications Engineering, University of Wollongong, Wollongong, New South Wales 2522, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical, Computer and Telecommunications Engineering, University of Wollongong, Wollongong, New South Wales, 2522, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Grid Gaoqing Power Supply Company, Zibo, Shandong 255000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Grid Gaoqing Power Supply Company

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Grid Gaoqing Power Supply Company, Zibo, Shandong, 255000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Precise estimating of the state of charge (SOC) in lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries is crucial for effective energy management and safety assurance in electric vehicles. This paper proposes a novel multi-scale joint estimation method for model parameters and SOC to enhance estimation accuracy under temperature variations and hysteresis effects. First, an improved second-order RC equivalent circuit model is developed by incorporating temperature dependencies and hysteresis effects, where the hysteresis parameters are calibrated offline using a data-driven approach. Then, the joint estimation approach employs an adaptive forgetting factor recursive least squares (AFFRLS) algorithm to dynamically update model parameters during SOC estimation, thereby maintaining model fidelity across diverse temperature conditions (−10 °C to 50 °C). Finally, an extended Kalman filter (EKF) is implemented for SOC estimation based on the real-time updated model parameters. Experimental validation under DST, US06, and FUDS conditions demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving a maximum voltage prediction error of 0.0650 and a maximum SOC estimation error of 0.0092 across the full temperature range.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries are fundamental to many different fields, especially in electric vehicles (EVs), since their lack of memory effect and superior environmental performance [1,2]. State of charge (SOC) estimation is an essential part of electric vehicle battery management systems (BMS). It helps prevent permanent damage to the battery's internal structure from over (dis)charging, assuring both security and efficiency [3].

Due to the vigorous electrochemical reactions occurring within the Li-ion batteries of electric vehicles during operation, sensors are unable to provide an exact direct measurement of SOC. Consequently, SOC can only be obtained indirectly through estimation methodologies. Currently, methods for SOC estimation are often roughly classified into data-driven estimation, open circuit voltage (OCV) approach, model-based estimation, and ampere-hour (Ah) integration [4,5]. Among these, The model-based approach is commonly utilized due to superior precision and interpretability [6,7], which is also employed in this study.

Model-based SOC estimation methods, including the extended Kalman filter (EKF) and adaptive EKF (AEKF), convert the internal battery variables into parameters of physical significance to estimate SOC [8,9]. These approaches are commonly adopted to estimate SOC since the fast convergence to true values, even when starting with an inaccurate pre-set SOC value. Compared to other models, the equivalent circuit model (ECM), structured by serially connected RC parallel branches, is a widely used model due to its well-designed structure and explainable physical parameters [10]. However, adding RC parallel branches in ECM leads to higher computational demands for parameter identification, which can be detrimental to its application in EV BMS [11]. Thus, the second-order RC models are adopted to depict the electrochemical characteristics of batteries because they effectively balance computational complexity and prediction accuracy [12]. For model-based methods, the estimation precision is highly dependent on the precision of model parameters. Thus, the model parameters need continuous correction and updating based on parameter identification results to adapt to changing operating conditions.

Accurate model parameter identification can timely reflect the battery's operating status and serves as the foundation for BMS SOC estimation [13]. Model parameter identification can be broadly categorized into online and offline approaches [14]. Offline identification methods are prone to significant SOC estimation errors due to external factors like ambient temperature and hysteresis effects [15,16]. In contrast, online identification methods are preferred for precise SOC estimation because they can effectively address these issues. The recursive least squares (RLS), along with its derivatives, are frequently utilized for real-time parameter identification because of their acceptable computational complexity and adequate precision [17]. However, RLS can accumulate data to the saturation point, causing new data to be significantly influenced by older data, leading to increased identification errors [18]. To mitigate this issue, the forgetting factor RLS (FFRLS) method is adopted to balance old and new data by adjusting a forgetting factor λ, thereby reducing parameter identification errors [19]. However, a constant forgetting factor struggles to track dynamically changing parameters, which can reduce performance in terms of stability and tracking ability [20,21]. To address this, the adaptive forgetting factor should be integrated with ECM-based online parameter identification to improve accuracy [22].

Changes in SOC during battery operation can cause a mismatch between pre-set model parameters and actual operating conditions, leading to estimation errors, particularly in certain SOC ranges, such as near the cutoff discharge region [23,24]. To enhance SOC estimation performance, a joint estimation method is needed that can estimate SOC and model parameters simultaneously. For instance, Ref. [25] proposes a joint estimation approach based on FFRLS combined with EKF for Li-ion batteries, which adjusts model parameters in response to SOC changes to improve accuracy. Similarly, Ref. [26] proposes a joint estimation method to minimize the errors of SOC estimation resulting from unknown system noise, using FFRLS for real-time identification of model parameters and AEKF for SOC updates. Therefore, incorporating SOC dependency into the battery model is reasonable and can effectively improve estimation accuracy.

During battery operation, parameter mismatches caused by temperature fluctuations can also lead to a decline in estimation accuracy. To address this, several studies considering battery temperature variations have been conducted. Ref. [27] proposes a temperature-based fractional first-order RC circuit model and introduces a dual fractional-order Kalman filter for SOC computation that accounts for temperature effects. Ref. [28] presents a second-order RC equivalent circuit model with temperature correction, enhancing model precision by adjusting estimated parameters. Ref. [29] introduces an improved multiple feature–electrochemical thermal coupling modeling method to improve battery estimation performance under low-temperature conditions. Ref. [30] develops an online modified square root–untraced Kalman filtering algorithm to analyze the impact of temperature-induced capacity fluctuations, employs multi-limit factor fusion to reduce complexity, and achieves full-parameter online identification to enhance estimation accuracy across multiple temperature conditions. Ref. [31] establishes a parameter optimization method for mobile estimation windows based on the particle swarm optimization–adaptive square root cubature Kalman filter to improve SOC estimation capability and accuracy. Therefore, dependencies on both SOC and temperature variations should be incorporated into estimation algorithms; however, this increases estimation complexity. Additionally, because model parameters change slowly over time compared to SOC, operating both on the same time scale also increases computational complexity [32].

Considering the impact of both temperature and SOC variations on estimation accuracy requires more advanced models or algorithms. Recent studies have introduced joint estimation approaches to improve SOC estimation accuracy. Due to the differing time-varying nature of parameters and SOC, Ref. [33] introduces an EKF-based method for estimating battery parameters and states, demonstrating enhanced SOC estimation precision with reduced computational time. Similarly, Ref. [34] proposes a multi-scale adaptive identification approach using a dual EKF (DEKF), effectively improving accuracy. Typically, the EKF is efficient and holds promise for integration into programmable logic controllers (PLCs) for industrial applications. However, the improved EKF methods, despite enhancing accuracy, significantly increase computational complexity, limiting their practical implementation.

Given this context, this paper aims to design a model-based estimation method that simultaneously considers the effects of temperature and SOC variations on estimation accuracy, without significantly increasing computational complexity. An improved ECM that accounts for both the hysteresis effect and temperature is proposed to ensure sufficient model accuracy. A multi-scale framework is adopted for the coupling of model parameters and SOC, considering their different time-varying characteristics. Model parameters are updated in real time during the estimation process using adaptive FFRLS. The goal is to achieve the simultaneous estimation of model parameters and SOC, ensuring accurate SOC estimation under various temperature conditions. The key contributions of this study are as follows:
(1)
An improved battery model is developed to characterize and compensate the impacts of temperature and hysteresis effect. This model is adaptable to different temperatures and SOCs while minimizing errors.
(2)
The offline calibration of the hysteresis factor based on neural networks is combined with the online identification of RC parameters using FFRLS with an adaptive forgetting factor λ, ensuring the accuracy of the battery model parameters as environmental conditions change.
(3)
A multi-scale joint estimation approach for battery parameters and SOC is introduced to address the slow-varying nature of battery characteristics and the fast-varying nature of SOC. By utilizing AFFRLS for macro-scale online parameter identification and EKF for micro-scale SOC estimation, this method improves estimation accuracy while ensuring acceptable computational complexity.

The OCV is one of the most critical parameters for power batteries, used to analyze variations in electron energy within electrode materials and to estimate the SOC. However, chemical reactions and physical processes inside Li-ion batteries introduce a hysteresis effect, causing the OCV-SOC relationship to differ between charging and discharging cycles. In model-based methods, SOC is typically determined using the OCV-SOC relationship. Nevertheless, under electric vehicle operating conditions, batteries undergo alternating charging and discharging cycles, even if the overall cycle appears to be in a discharge state. Traditional battery models employ a discharge OCV-SOC look-up table to determine an approximate SOC value. Nevertheless, the OCV-SOC curves shown in Fig. 1 processes exhibit significant discrepancies during charge and discharge. In such cases, if the battery is charging, using the discharge OCV-SOC to determine SOC will result in estimation errors. Consequently, the hysteresis effect on battery OCV must be carefully accounted for to ensure accurate SOC estimation.
To eliminate the hysteresis effect, we introduce an independent series voltage source UH in the modeling process to enhance the accuracy of the model, as shown in Fig. 2 .

The working principle is when a charging current is detected, the compensation voltage is produced to mitigate the end voltage prediction errors due to using discharge OCV-SOC only. Since the OCV-SOC dependence for (dis)charging varies with temperature, we incorporate temperature dependence into UH ​. Consequently, UH ​ (T, Z) is determined based on both temperature T and SOC, denoted as Z.

Li-ion batteries for EVs exhibit time-varying and nonlinear characteristics during charging and discharging. Thus, a second-order RC model is adopted to capture the characterization, as the two parallel RC structures provide sufficient functional expansion without significantly increasing computational complexity [24]. Additionally, ambient temperature critically affects the OCV-SOC relationship, as visualized in Fig. 3 . Temperatures can accelerate or decelerate the diffusion rate of Li-ions within the battery, leading to dynamic changes in electrochemical reactions, which are reflected in the battery model parameters.

Consequently, the impacts of ambient temperature on model parameter identification during the battery modeling process should be considered. Therefore, the resistance and capacitance parameters in the proposed model have been made dependent on temperature (T) and SOC (Z), resulting in model parameters that vary under different temperature conditions. This approach allows for the simulation of battery characteristics across varying temperatures. Considering the battery model parameters influenced by temperature, SOC, and hysteresis, this paper presents an improved RC model, as shown in Fig. 2.

The variables within parentheses shown in Fig. 2 indicate that these parameters change with the respective variables, where T represents the temperature and Z represents the SOC. UL represents the battery terminal voltage, UH (T, Z) represents a voltage source for the hysteresis effect, I represents the current, and UOC represents the OCV. Moreover, C 1 and C 2 represent the polarization capacitance, R 0 and U 0 are the ohmic resistance and its voltage, respectively, R 1 and R 2 are the polarization resistance, and the voltage of RC parallel branches are denoted as U 1 and U 2.

Based on the improved model, the mathematical equation of Li-ion batteries applying Kirchhoff’s law is shown in Eq. (1), in which R 0, R 1, R 2, C 1, and C 2 are adopted to depict Li-ion batteries’ temperature and SOC characteristics.
(1)
U 0
=
R 0
( T , Z )

U ̇
1
=
I

( T , Z )

U 1

( T , Z )
C 1
( T , Z )

2
=
I
C 2
( T , Z )
-
U 2
R 2
( T , Z )
C 2
( T , Z )
U L
=
U
OC
+
U H
( T , Z )

U 1

-
U 0
= O C V -
U 1
-
U 2
-
U 0
In this model, U 0 simulates transient voltage drops, U 1​ represents short-term polarization caused by internal charge transfer, and U 2​ accounts for long-term polarization due to diffusion processes. To mitigate hysteresis effects, an additional voltage source UH is incorporated. By introducing temperature and SOC dependencies into U 0​, U 1​, U 2​, and UH ​, the model achieves improved dynamic accuracy across different temperature and SOC conditions. The expression for the OCV, consisting of UOC and UH ​, is provided in Eq. (2).
(2)
OCV =
U
oc , d i s
( T , Z )
+
U H
( T , Z )
=
U
oc , d i s
( T , Z )
+ μ
U
oc , c h
( T , Z )

U
oc , d i s
( T , Z )
=
μ
×
U
oc , c h
( T , Z )
+
( 1 -
μ
)
×
U
oc , d i s
( T , Z )
where Uoc,dis and Uoc,ch are the OCV during discharging and charging, respectively, and μ is the hysteresis factor.

Since the battery operates predominantly in discharge mode, we consider only the discharge condition in our studies. Under this condition, a smaller μ value causes the OCV to more closely approximate the actual discharge characteristic, thereby requiring minimal compensation for hysteresis voltage. Conversely, a larger μ value necessitates greater compensation for hysteresis voltage to simulate the real operating conditions accurately. The μ is dependent on the accumulation of the SOC that is calculated by the current during (dis)charging, weakening the impact of the hysteresis effect [35]. The expression for μ is presented in Eq. (3).
(3)
μ k
=
μ
k - 1
-
(
I k
× Δ T
η
C n
)
1 β

In Eq. (3), the subscript k, k-1 denotes the time, μk , μk-
1 denote the hysteresis factor, Ik denotes the current, ΔT denotes the sampling time, η denotes the coulomb efficiency, and Cn denotes the rated capacity of the batteries, β represents SOC the accumulation factor. As shown in Eq. (2), temperature and SOC dependencies are incorporated into the OCV model to enhance its dynamic accuracy. Therefore, the hysteresis factor μ should also depend on temperature and SOC. To calibrate μ, the hysteresis voltage is first plotted at various temperatures and SOC levels, as illustrated in Fig. 4 .

As presented in Fig. 4, hysteresis voltage rises rapidly when the SOC approaches 0 and 1, and the entire 3D response surface exhibits significant nonlinearity. To address this nonlinear behavior, we employed data-driven methods to generate a 3D lookup table for μ based on temperature and SOC. This enables the algorithm to directly reference the appropriate μ value from the lookup table to obtain hysteresis voltage correction when predicting the battery terminal voltage.

The performance of three data-driven approaches—Support Vector Regression (SVR), Random Forest, and Neural Network—was initially evaluated using 80 % of the dataset for training and the remaining 20 % for validation. The dataset comprises data from Low-current OCV tests conducted on A123 LiFePO4 Li-ion batteries (M1A) over a temperature range of −10 °C to 50 °C, in 10 °C increments. The Low-current OCV test acquires an approximation of the corresponding terminal voltage by applying a small current to the battery. The procedure for the Low-current test is as follows:
i)
Charge the battery to the cut-off voltage at a constant current of 1C-rate.
ii)
Charge at a constant voltage until the current decreases to 0.01C.
iii)
Discharge at a constant rate of C/20 until the voltage drops to the cut-off voltage.
iv)
Fully charge at a constant rate of C/20.

At low currents, the terminal voltage closely approximates the OCV. By comparing the terminal voltages obtained during the discharge and charge processes, the true values of hysteresis voltage under corresponding SOC and temperature conditions can be calculated. Subsequently, the performance of the three data-driven methods is assessed. The experimental current, voltage, temperature, and SOC are inputs, and the predicted hysteresis voltage is the output. The algorithm’s performance is evaluated by comparing the predicted values with the experimentally obtained true values. The prediction results and residuals for the three methods are presented in Fig. 5 .

Fig. 5 illustrates that the Neural Network demonstrates superior performance. The Mean Squared Error (MSE) are also used to assess the numerical results of hysteresis voltage predictions using the above three methods, as presented in Table 1 .

The numerical results further confirm the Neural Network’s outstanding performance in addressing this issue. Consequently, we adopted the Neural Network to generate the 3D lookup table for μ. Initially, the Neural Network was trained using battery charging and discharging data at −10 °C ∼ 50 °C with 10 °C intervals as inputs and μ as outputs. The training results are presented in Fig. 6 .

Subsequently, the trained network was utilized to create the 3D lookup table for μ. This offline-generated lookup table is employed by the algorithm for online voltage estimation, significantly reducing the computational demands of online calculations. Additionally, the visualization of the μ 3D response surface is provided in Fig. 7 .

After calibrating the μ values, the Ah method was applied to calculate the SOC, as shown in Eq. (4).
(4)
Z t
=
Z
t 0

η

∫
t 0
t
I ( τ )
d τ

Since SOC varies continuously but is estimated in discrete time due to sensor limitations, the proposed model needs to be discretized. First, the equations for the two RC parallel branches in Eq. (1), U 1​ and U 2​​, are discretized to obtain the corresponding discrete zero-state response and zero-input response equations, as shown in Eq. (5) and Eq. (6).

Zero-state response equation:

Next, Eq. (4) is discretized and then combined with Eq. (5) and Eq. (6) to derive the discrete state-space equations of the proposed model, as presented in Eq. (7). The corresponding discrete equation for the battery terminal voltage U L is provided in Eq. (8).

0 , k

At this point, the mathematical formulation and discretization of the battery model have been completed. The proposed model incorporates battery hysteresis effects into the modeling process. Additionally, the dynamic parameters in the model are dependent on temperature and SOC, ensuring higher accuracy under different operating conditions.

The OCV required for parameter identification is derived from accurate OCV-SOC curves. If the model parameters used in SOC estimation are biased, it can lead to a substantial deviation of the SOC estimation from actual values, resulting in a persistent steady-state bias that cannot be eliminated. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a joint estimation framework that simultaneously estimates both model parameters and SOCs for improving estimation accuracy.

The model parameters exhibit slow time-varying characteristics, whereas SOC changes rapidly. Additionally, performing parameter identification at every time step requires substantial computational resources. To address this issue, we propose a multi-scale approach for battery parameter identification and SOC estimation. This approach involves conducting parameter identification at the macro-scale and updating the battery model parameters to better reflect battery characteristics. When the absolute change in SOC at the micro-scale exceeds a certain threshold, the model parameters are adjusted at the macro-scale.

The Li-ion battery exemplifies its nonlinear behavior under real operating conditions. Concerning the forgetting factor λ in RLS-based methods, utilizing a constant λ for model parameter identification may cause dynamic parameter changes to diverge from their true values due to various influencing factors, resulting in decreased algorithm stability. To address this issue, the AFFRLS method is proposed. Since battery model parameters vary slowly over time, identifying these parameters at the same rate as SOC estimation provides limited improvement in estimation accuracy and increases algorithm complexity and computation time. Thus, battery model parameter identification is conducted at longer time intervals (i.e., at the macro scale). First, the AFFRLS parameter estimation at this scale is defined, as represented by Eq. (9).
(9)
y
L , k
=
φ
L , k
T
θ
L , k
+
e
L , k
where the subscript L indicates that the calculation process is carried out at the macro-scale, yL,k is the system output, φL,k denotes data vector, θL,k denotes estimated parameter vector, and eL,k denotes white noise with zero means. Next, the adaptive forgetting factor λ is incorporated into the AFFRLS method through an adaptive equation. The expression for the adaptive forgetting factor λ is given in Eq. (10).
(10)
λ
L , k
=
λ
L , min
+
( 1 -
λ
L , min
)
h
g
L , k
g
L , k
= r o u n d
(
(
e
L , k
e
L , r
)
2
)
where λL,
min represents the minimum value of λ, h represents the sensitivity factor, round(n) represents an integer close to n, eL,r represents the allowable error base, and eL,k represents estimation error at time k. λ adjusts dynamically based on terminal voltage prediction, thereby enhancing the accuracy of model. When the prediction error is small, λ is kept close to 1 to maintain stability in parameter identification. Conversely, when the prediction error is large, λ is reduced to improve model precision and the convergence speed. Additionally, the algorithm continuously optimizes λ to a suitable value as the parameter identification approaches a steady state. The recursion formula of the AFFRLS method, incorporating the adaptive forgetting factor, are shown in Eq. (11).
(11)
θ ̂
L , k
=
θ ̂
L , k - 1
+
K

In Eq. (11),
θ ̂
L , k
and
θ ̂
L , k - 1
denote the parameter estimation at time k and time k-1, respectively, KL,k denotes the gain matrix,
y
L , k
denotes the observed value, and
P
L , k
denotes the covariance matrix. After constructing the recursive equations, the terminal voltage and OCV in the discrete state-space equation correspond to the system output y in Eq. (9), with current I as the input and R 0, R 1, R 2, C 1, and C 2​ as the parameters θ to be identified. During the parameter identification process, the terminal voltage calculated based on the assumed identification results is compared with the predicted voltage, and the adaptive forgetting factor λ is adjusted according to Eq. (10). Since the proposed model incorporates hysteresis, temperature, and SOC dependencies, the high voltage prediction accuracy ensures that the adaptive updating of λ is more effective, enabling the AFFRLS method to quickly track parameter variations while maintaining steady-state accuracy.

The AFFRLS method at the macro-scale is integrated into the joint estimation framework to enable necessary real-time model parameter updates during the estimation process, thereby improving estimation accuracy. A more detailed discussion is provided in Section 3.3.

The standard Kalman filter (KF) shows limited accuracy in discrete systems, while the EKF is commonly used to handle nonlinear systems by applying a Taylor series expansion, which linearizes the state-space model. Given that the BMS typically requires SOC estimation at the same sampling frequency as the current sensor (per second), we propose using the EKF for SOC estimation at the micro-scale. In Eq. (12), the state-space model of the nonlinear discrete system is depicted.
(12)
x

In Eq. (12), the subscript l denotes the micro-scale,
x
l , k
denotes the state variable,
u
l , k
denotes the system output variable,
w
l . k
represents measurement noise,
y
l , k
denotes the measurement variable, and
v
l , k
denotes the noise.
The fundamental formulas of the EKF method are shown in Eqs. (13)- (15).

l , k

The EKF integrated into the joint estimation process can utilize real-time updated model parameters during each iteration to ensure high estimation accuracy at relatively low computational cost, while maintaining stability by updating the covariance matrix.

The Li-ion battery’s SOC exhibits fast-varying characteristics, whereas its model parameters do not require frequent adjustment since environmental changes are relatively slow. To enhance the precision of estimation without adding unnecessary computational complexity, a multi-scale joint estimation method is presented to estimate both parameters and SOC. In this approach, the AFFRLS is adopted for identifying the key parameters at the macro-scale, such as polarization resistance, ohmic resistance, and polarization capacitance, while the EKF is employed for estimating the SOC at the micro-scale. Fig. 8 shows the block diagram of the proposed multi-scale joint estimation.

In Fig. 8, the micro-scale l represents the SOC estimation with its fast-varying characteristic, and the macro-scale L represents the pa0rameter identification with its slow-varying characteristic. The EKF ensures that SOC is estimated at each time step, while the AFFRLS is triggered for parameter re-identification when an increase in the frequency of charging current and temperature changes is detected, thereby maintaining estimation accuracy. The basic steps of the multi-scale joint estimation are shown below:
(1)
Set the initial value of the system.
(2)
Adopt AFFRLS for parameter identification for obtaining the initial values.
(3)
Apply EKF for micro-scale SOC estimation. Utilizing the online identification of model parameter utilizing the AFFRLS, the matrix parameters of the measurement and state equations in the EKF algorithm are updated. Then, SOC is estimated using the EKF using the real-time updated model.
(4)
Compare the micro-scale l with the macro-scale L. If l > L, the model parameters are identified using AFFRLS at macro-scale, and then repeat step (3). Otherwise, continue to estimate SOC utilizing the EKF at the micro-scale until the judging condition, l > L, is satisfied.
(5)
Update micro-scale l. Compare the parameter μ and temperature based on the definition in Eq. (2), when μ = 0, it indicates that a discharging current is applied at that moment. If μ ≠ 0, it indicates that a charging current is applied at that moment. Even with the inclusion of an independent series voltage source in the model to mitigate errors caused by the hysteresis effect, the charging current at that moment remains a potential source of estimation error. Therefore, a condition is added such that if μ ≠ 0, the counter l is incremented by 1. This strategy ensures consistent parameter update frequency, thereby reducing estimation errors. Additionally, an “or ΔTemp > ζ” criterion is incorporated: when the temperature continuously changes, l is also incremented by 1. This ensures that the model parameters are updated to fit the current temperature, maintaining accuracy under varying temperature conditions. When setting the temperature threshold ζ, the specific application environment should be considered to choose an appropriate threshold, ensuring that when μ ≠ 0 occurs continuously, the model parameters can also be updated through the temperature-dependent criterion.

Here is an example calculation demonstrating the typical process for determining the temperature threshold. Considering the standard operating temperature range of 0–50 °C, the temperature criterion can be triggered at most 50/ζ times, meaning the model parameters are updated at least 50/(L × ζ) times. Assuming that frequent updates are to be avoided, L is set to 30 and 20 model updates are desired, which yields ζ = 0.083. Since the first, μ-dependent criterion also contributes to the increment of l, ζ is set to 0.1, which should satisfy the requirements. Under conditions of minimal temperature variation, an increased ζ should be considered to reduce the model update frequency and preserve accuracy; conversely, in extreme environmental conditions, a decreased ζ should be adopted to increase the update frequency and ensure accuracy. Selecting an appropriate ζ value in specific applications ensures that the model is updated promptly without imposing excessive computational burdens.

The micro-scale l is initialized to 0, and the macro-scale L can be adjusted to optimize the joint estimation. For instance, in the multi-scale AFFRLS-EKF joint estimation method, the macro-scale sampling time is set to 10. Consequently, after accumulating 10 instances of charging current events or temperature changes, a parameter update is performed. This strategy ensures a certain parameter update frequency, thereby enhancing the accuracy of SOC estimation without significantly increasing computational complexity.

This paper employs an A123 LiFePO4 Li-ion battery (M1A) for testing, with the basic parameters listed in Table 2 . The experimental configuration includes a temperature-controlled chamber and a BTS-20 battery testing system, as shown in Fig. 9 . The thermal chamber is utilized to simulate the environmental temperatures, while the BTS-20 applies various current profiles to the battery and collects experimental data. The collected data serve as true values for comparison with the simulation results, validating the performance of the multi-scale AFFRLS-EKF joint estimation method. The simulations were conducted on a system equipped with an AMD 5900X CPU and 32 GB of RAM.

Experiments were conducted under three different driving cycles—Dynamic Stress Test (DST), US06 Driving Cycle (US06), and Federal Urban Driving Schedule (FUDS)—and at three temperature conditions: −10 °C, 30 °C, and 50 °C. These three temperatures correspond to low, ambient, and high-temperature environments, respectively. Fig. 10 depicts the battery voltage and current data of these conditions.

To assess the proposed approach, a comparison between the baseline EKF and the proposed multi-scale AFFRLS-EKF is conducted. In the simulation, since the baseline EKF cannot update parameter identification results during the estimation process, the parameter identification results at room temperature (30°C) are used as the model parameters. For the multi-scale AFFRLS-EKF method, the micro-scale l is initialized to 0, the temperature threshold ζ is set to 0.1, and the macro-scale L is set to 30.

Based on the established model, AFFRLS is employed for parameter identification. To illustrate how the parameters vary with temperature and SOC more clearly, the identification results are visualized across different ambient temperatures (−10 °C to 50 °C) and SOC levels (approximately 0 % to 100 %), as shown in Fig. 11 . The visualization includes identification results at discrete temperatures of − 10 °C, 0 °C, 10 °C, 20 °C, 25 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C, and 50 °C, as well as at temperatures where the model parameters are updated during the estimation process. Since these results do not cover all possible temperatures, linear interpolation is applied to generate a complete 3D response surface, providing a clearer representation of parameter evolution with SOC and temperature.

As shown in Fig. 11, the parameter values vary across different temperatures and SOC, exhibiting pronounced nonlinear characteristics. Moreover, the model parameter curves fluctuate with changes in SOC even at a constant temperature. Therefore, neglecting the impact of SOC variations on model parameters reduces model accuracy and introduces significant errors in SOC estimation. This highlights the necessity of real-time SOC updates during model parameter identification to ensure estimation accuracy. The proposed joint estimation method enables necessary model parameter updates during SOC estimation, ensuring that the parameters are adjusted to the corresponding temperature and SOC conditions. As a result, even when using a basic EKF for SOC estimation, high accuracy can still be achieved due to the incorporation of a high-precision model.

The proposed model accounts for hysteresis effects and effectively tracks the battery terminal voltage across different temperatures. Therefore, it is essential to examine the voltage prediction results during the SOC estimation process. The voltage measurement results and corresponding errors under three driving cycles—DST, US06, and FUDS—and at three temperature conditions—−10 °C, 30 °C, and 50 °C—are presented in Fig. 12 through Fig. 14. Due to the presence of several thousand-time steps in each driving cycle, selected portions of each graph have been magnified to provide a clearer demonstration of the results. For each temperature condition, the results are displayed in two images with a total of four subfigures: Figures (a), (c), and (e) show the comparison between the predicted and actual voltage values, with magnified sections offering a clearer view of the details. Meanwhile, Figures (b), (d), and (f) illustrate the voltage prediction errors along with their magnified sections.

As illustrated in Figs. 12 through 14
, under all tested conditions, the AFFRLS-EKF method consistently exhibits lower voltage prediction errors, with errors approaching ± 0.01 V. In contrast, the baseline EKF method demonstrates significant fluctuations in voltage prediction accuracy. From the magnified voltage prediction graphs, it can be observed that the AFFRLS-EKF method closely follows the actual voltage values, whereas the baseline EKF method shows increased prediction errors, especially during rapid voltage variations. Voltage prediction errors directly impact the accuracy of the model, leading to increased SOC estimation errors. Furthermore, estimation errors accumulate over time. While the EKF partially corrects this accumulated error in SOC estimation, using the ampere-hour integration method would result in progressively larger estimation errors over time. To provide a more intuitive representation of the estimation results, the corresponding numerical results are presented in Tables3-5
. Root mean squared error (RMSE) and mean relative error (MRE) are utilized to show numerical results, evaluating the performance of the model, as shown in Eq. (17) and Eq. (18).
(17)
RMSE =
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
(
x i
-
X i
)
2
(18)
MRE =
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
x i
-
X i
X i
where Xi is the experimental data, xi is the estimation and n is the total amount of data. Tables 3–5 clearly demonstrate that the AFFRLS-EKF method consistently outperforms the baseline EKF, exhibiting lower errors across various operating conditions and temperatures. Notably, voltage prediction errors are reduced by approximately 70 %. As discussed in Section 2: Modeling, model accuracy is significantly influenced by temperature and SOC variations. The numerical results highlight that the baseline EKF experiences a substantial increase in errors under both low- and high-temperature conditions, with errors doubling or even exceeding those observed at normal temperatures. In contrast, the proposed method dynamically updates model parameters in response to environmental changes, effectively mitigating the impact of temperature fluctuations on prediction accuracy. Even under extreme temperature conditions, the estimation error remains comparable to that observed at normal temperatures. Furthermore, across all tested temperatures and driving cycles, the AFFRLS-EKF method consistently delivers superior performance. These findings confirm that the proposed model is a high-precision, adaptive approach capable of dynamically adjusting to environmental conditions while maintaining accurate voltage predictions across diverse scenarios.

To comprehensively assess the estimation precision of the proposed method, the SOCs under various driving conditions and temperatures are estimated using both the baseline EKF and the multi-scale AFFRLS-EKF joint estimation methods. The results are presented in Fig. 15 through17. To enhance the clarity of the estimation results, magnified insets were included below the SOC estimation graphs, and estimation error plots were provided. These error plots facilitate a clear comparison of the performance differences between the baseline EKF and the AFFRLS-EKF methods.

The error results in Figs. 15–17
highlight the superior performance of the proposed method. It can be observed that, under various temperature and operating conditions, the SOC estimation results of the proposed method are consistently closer to the true SOC values compared to the baseline EKF. Additionally, the error plots on the right further demonstrate that the estimation errors of the proposed method remain consistently lower than those of the baseline EKF across the entire evaluation period. Notably, the estimation error of the proposed method remains nearly constant, staying within 0.5 %, whereas the baseline EKF exhibits error accumulation over time, with the maximum estimation error exceeding 2 %. This confirms the effectiveness of the proposed strategy, which enhances SOC estimation accuracy by incorporating temperature and hysteresis dependencies into an improved second-order RC equivalent circuit model. Furthermore, since the model integrates online parameter identification, it is essential to evaluate its computational resource consumption. The numerical results of error analysis and runtime are presented in Tables 6–8
. Among these, the “average time cost” represents the total time required to complete the simulation of an entire driving cycle.

The results in Tables 6–8 indicate that at a temperature of 30 °C, the baseline EKF and AFFRLS-EKF methods exhibit the most comparable performance across the three driving cycles. This is because the baseline EKF utilizes parameter identification values obtained at 30 °C. However, as the temperature decreases to − 10 °C or increases to 50 °C, the mismatch between the fixed model parameters and the actual environment causes a significant increase in estimation errors for the baseline EKF. In contrast, the AFFRLS-EKF method can update model parameters in real-time, resulting in only a slight increase in SOC estimation errors. This contrast in error variation across different operating temperatures highlights the feasibility of improving estimation accuracy by dynamically updating model parameters to accommodate changing conditions.

Furthermore, the computational time of the proposed method does not increase significantly. Compared to the baseline EKF, which requires approximately 4 s for computation, the AFFRLS-EKF method incurs an additional computational cost of about 1 s. Given that processing thousands of seconds of driving cycle data takes less than 5 s in total, the per-second computational resource consumption remains minimal. This efficiency is attributed to the multi-scale approach, which prevents frequent parameter updates, thereby avoiding a significant increase in computational time while still achieving notable improvements in SOC estimation accuracy.

An additional independent experiment is conducted to evaluate the convergence of the algorithm. Tests are performed under the US06 cycle at 50 °C and the DST cycle at −10 °C. Both the baseline EKF and AFFRLS-EKF are initialized with an incorrect SOC value of 80 % (while the actual SOC is approximately 100 %). The results are presented in Fig. 18 , where the SOC estimation during the first 300 s is magnified to highlight the convergence process. AFFRLS-EKF converges to the true SOC within 100 s, whereas the baseline EKF requires over 200 s. This demonstrates the superior convergence speed of the proposed method, attributed to its ability to update model parameters for improved accuracy.

Additionally, the performance of the proposed method is compared with those presented in three other papers. To ensure consistency, all experiments were conducted under DST conditions. All four methods considered the influence of temperature on estimation performance, and RMSE was used as the error metric for comparison. The numerical results are summarized in Table 9 .

The proposed method achieves an RMSE of 0.36 %, which is comparable to the 0.223 % reported in [29] and the 0.19 % in [31]. Both our approach and [29] support real-time parameter updates. However, the multiple feature-electrochemical thermal coupling modeling method in [29] is specifically tailored for low-temperature conditions, offering a more detailed model at the expense of increased computational complexity. Ref. [31] employs particle swarm optimization to determine the optimal adaptive estimation window and utilizes the more advanced—though computationally demanding—adaptive square root cubature Kalman filter, leading to a lower estimation error. Compared to [30], both methods incorporate online parameter identification to enhance model accuracy. However, the proposed method explicitly accounts for hysteresis effects and temperature dependence to minimize estimation errors, whereas [30] integrates state-of-power estimation to improve accuracy. The RMSE of the method in [30] is 0.84 %, slightly higher than the other three methods. In summary, despite utilizing a basic EKF for SOC estimation, the proposed method achieves accuracy comparable to other advanced approaches.

The proposed method enhances estimation accuracy by integrating hysteresis and temperature effects into the modeling process. However, for ease of practical implementation, EKF—chosen for its suitability for PLC programming—is used for estimation, leading to slightly higher errors compared to the most advanced estimation algorithms. Overall, the proposed method strikes a balance between model complexity and accuracy requirements, achieving high-precision SOC estimation with relatively low real-time computational resource demands.

The influence of the time scale on SOC estimation results exists. Different initial values of the micro and macro time-scales are investigated. Taking the US06 result at 50 °C as an example, the micro time-scale l is set to 1, while the macro time-scale L is varied among 10, 30, and 60 to compare the impact of the time scale on the algorithm’s performance. Fig. 19 presents a comparison of voltage prediction errors for the AFFRLS-EKF algorithms. It can be observed that the voltage prediction accuracy improves as L decreases, since a smaller L leads to more frequent model updates, albeit at the cost of increased computational burden.

Fig. 20 displays a comparison of SOC estimations for the AFFRLS-EKF algorithms. The results indicate that a smaller L yields SOC estimations that are closer to the true SOC values. The difference between the algorithms with L = 60 and L = 30 is particularly significant, as reducing L from 60 to 30 leads to noticeably improved estimation accuracy. In contrast, the results for L = 10 and L = 30 are similar, suggesting that once L is reduced to a certain threshold, further reductions do not contribute significantly to additional accuracy improvements. Fig. 21 , which presents a comparison of SOC estimation errors, further confirms these observations.

Furthermore, the numerical results are also summarized in Table 10 . Both the voltage prediction errors and the SOC estimation errors of the AFFRLS-EKF are lower than those of the baseline EKF, and the performance of the AFFRLS-EKF improves as L decreases. Notably, when L is reduced from 30 to 10, the error decreases by approximately 20 %, while the computation time increases from 4.74 s to 12.61 s. In cases where the error is already sufficiently low, further reduction of L may not be justified. Conversely, when L increases from 30 to 60, the error increases several-fold, yet the computation time decreases by only 0.55 s. These observations indicate that the selection of L is crucial, and the optimal value must be determined through tuning to balance accuracy and computational complexity.

This paper introduces a multi-scale joint estimation method that simultaneously identifies battery parameters and estimates SOC. An improved model has been developed by incorporating temperature and hysteresis dependencies to enhance accuracy under varying conditions. The proposed multi-scale joint scheme, combining AFFRLS-based parameter identification with EKF-based SOC estimation, enables real-time model parameter updates, which in turn allow for accurate SOC estimation using EKF. The effectiveness of this method is validated under DST, US06, and FUDS conditions at temperatures ranging from −10 °C to 50 °C. The results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves low voltage prediction errors and SOC estimation errors across various conditions, while also ensuring convergence and adaptability. Furthermore, the improved method significantly enhances estimation accuracy with minimal additional computational cost. In conclusion, the proposed approach dynamically updates model parameters, improves SOC estimation accuracy, and maintains computational efficiency, offering a practical solution for SOC estimation under temperature variations. In the future, the potential integration of the proposed method into PLCs will be further investigated to evaluate its industrial applications.

Xinhui Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenyuan Bai: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shuyu Xie: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jiatong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Danny Sutanto: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Kashem M. Muttaqi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
